title: Solus Christus
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro

outline.point: I. The Reformation's Recovery of Christ Alone
subpoints: A. The five solas and their context — Sola Scriptura leads to seeing Christ as the all-sufficient Savior
B. The Roman Catholic sacramental system: seven works required alongside faith — baptism, confirmation, Eucharist, penance, extreme unction, holy orders, matrimony
C. The math of salvation: Christ plus nothing equals everything; Christ plus something equals nothing
D. The apostles in Acts 4 were saying nothing new — they were restating what the New Testament had always taught

outline.point: II. Peter's Defense Before the Sanhedrin (Acts 4:1-11)
subpoints: A. Peter and John heal a lame man, preach the resurrection, and are arrested (Acts 3-4:4)
B. The Sanhedrin tries them for healing in the name of Jesus — the same pattern as 1st century Judaism adding performance requirements to faith
C. Peter's counterattack: what we did was good and done in the name of Jesus, whom you crucified and God raised
D. The proof of Christ's all-sufficiency: the empty tomb — neither the apostles nor the enemies could produce the body
A. 'Nor is there salvation in any other, for there is no other name under heaven given among men by which we must be saved'
B. John Newton's dying words: 'I am a great sinner and Christ is a great Savior'

C. 11 things we add to Christ instead of trusting him alone: religious practices, rule-keeping, denominational tradition, family pedigree, intellect, education, natural abilities, social media image, possessions, ethnicity, and sadness/penance

outline.point: IV. Christ Is Precious to Those Who Believe (1 Peter 2:4-7)
subpoints: A. To those who believe, Christ is precious — rejected by men but chosen and precious to God
B. Nothing can be added to his perfections and completed work — in him all guilt is cancelled
C. The answer to every tendency to add to Christ: 'there is no other name under heaven by which we must be saved'

summary:
The second sermon in an October Reformation series, this message on Solus Christus takes up Acts 4:1-12 as the New Testament foundation for the Reformation doctrine that Christ alone saves. Pastor Lyro begins by tracing the historical path from the Bible's supreme authority (Sola Scriptura) to its inevitable conclusion: an all-sufficient Savior. The early church, like the Reformers, found itself surrounded by a religious system that added human performance to the work of Christ — first-century Judaism's extra-biblical requirements mirroring the Roman Catholic sacramental system the Reformers challenged.

The sermon's centerpiece is Peter's bold speech before the Sanhedrin: what we did was good, we did it in the name of Jesus whom you crucified, and God approved of him by raising him from the dead — the empty tomb being the strongest apologetic for the gospel. From Acts 4:12 — "there is no other name under heaven by which we must be saved" — the sermon expands into a searching pastoral application. Eleven things that Christians commonly add to Christ in place of trusting him alone are named and dismantled: religious observances, moral record-keeping, denominational identity, family pedigree, intellect, education, natural abilities, social media image, possessions, ethnicity, and even sadness or penance. The sermon closes with 1 Peter 2, where the same Peter who spoke in Acts 4 later writes that to those who believe, Christ is precious — the cornerstone rejected by men but upheld by God.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: Acts 4:1-12

If you're able to remain standing for the reading of God's Word, we're going to be reading from Acts chapter 4, as we continue with our Reformation series for the month of October. Acts chapter 4, verses 1 through 12. This is the word of our Lord.

Now as they spoke to the people, the priests, the captain of the temple, and the Sadducees came upon them, being greatly disturbed that they taught the people and preached in Jesus the resurrection from the dead, and they laid hands on them and put them in custody until the next day, for it was already evening. However, many of those who heard the word believed, and the number of the men came to be about five thousand. And it came to pass on the next day, that their rulers, elders, and scribes, as well as Annas the high priest, Caiaphas, John, and Alexander, and as many as were of the family of the high priest, were gathered together at Jerusalem. And when they had set them in the midst, they asked, "By what power or by what name have you done this?" Then Peter, filled with the Holy Spirit, said to them, "Rulers of the people and elders of Israel: if we this day are judged for a good deed done to a helpless man, by what means he has been made well, let it be known to you, and to all the people of Israel, that by the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth, whom you crucified, whom God raised from the dead, by him this man stands here before you whole. This is the stone which was rejected by you builders, which has become the chief cornerstone. Nor is there salvation in any other, for there is no other name under heaven given among men, by which we must be saved."

This is God's glorious Word. Let us pray together. Father, we pray that you would open our eyes to see wonderful things concerning your word this morning. We pray that your Spirit preach a much better sermon than I can, and that you would reach the hearts of all who are here. We pray in Jesus' name, amen.

## Introduction

Five hundred years ago, in God's good providence — because everything happens in God's good providence, and it's all good, even when it's painful — He brought a great revival to His church. Today we call that revival the Protestant Reformation of the 16th century. It was the greatest revival outside the Book of Acts. There had never been a greater revival till then, and there has never been a greater revival in the Church of Jesus Christ since then.

Though it was spread throughout all of Europe, all the reformers' ideas came together around five pillars — five foundational blocks on which all of Reformation theology was built:

- The Bible is our only and sufficient authority in all matters of faith and practice.
- The work of salvation is a completed work, finished at the cross and in the resurrection of Christ.
- Faith is the only thing required for salvation.
- Christ is the only Savior of mankind.
- All these things happen for the glory of God alone.

Last week we took a look at the idea that the scriptures alone are our final and supreme authority over all matters of faith and practice. And when you look at history, any and all revivals include a return to the Word of God as sufficient revelation. As the church goes back to the Word, she sees in it an all-sufficient Savior — the Lord Jesus Christ, the only one who saves.

## I. The Reformation's Recovery of Christ Alone

Having the courage to be a Protestant requires the courage to see Christ as the all-sufficient Savior — a Christ who, by virtue of who he is (truly God and truly man) and by virtue of what he has done (his perfect life of obedience, his criminal's death, his resurrection, his ascension, and his session at the right hand of the Father), is the all-sufficient Savior of all God's elect.

This is a concept the Church of Jesus Christ struggles with perennially. We are always struggling with the exclusivity of Jesus Christ. We like adding things to him. And this is what led eventually to the Protestant Reformation. By the time the 1500s arrived, the church had developed a whole system of things you were supposed to add to Christ in order to make it to heaven — what we call the sacramental system. Seven actions that you had to do in order to attain grace from God:

**Baptism** — believed to forgive original sin and all sins up to the point of baptism. **Confirmation** — required before participating in the Lord's Supper. **The Eucharist** — necessary to partake often of, or you had no hope of heaven. **Penance** — auricular confession to a priest, followed by prescribed acts of penitence. That's where indulgences came in — you could buy a piece of paper to fulfill your penance without actually doing it. That's what drove Luther to nail his 95 Theses to the castle church door. **Extreme unction** — anointing by a priest at death; without it, no hope of heaven. **Holy orders** — ordination to ministry. **Matrimony** — the seventh sacrament.

These were developed in addition to faith in Christ. But with time, they virtually replaced faith in Christ in the practice of the common people.

The math of salvation: Christ plus nothing equals everything. Christ plus something equals nothing. Christ plus nothing equals heaven. Christ plus something equals hell. That's the math the scriptures give concerning our salvation.

The reformers were not saying anything new. They were simply stating what the apostles had stated in the New Testament. And that's exactly what we see in Acts 4.

## II. Peter's Defense Before the Sanhedrin (Acts 4:1-11)

Peter and John had healed a lame man in the temple — walking and leaping and praising God. That led to a lot of attention, to Peter preaching his second recorded sermon, and to thousands being saved. The religious leaders, upset by this, arrested Peter and John.

The first-century Jews had also added all kinds of performance requirements to faith in God — to the point that faith in God was completely obscured. The apostles found themselves in essentially the same position as the Reformers in the 1500s. And why do we find this repetition? Why do we read this here in Acts, and earlier in the Old Testament as well? Because this is a tendency of the human heart. We tend to add things to what God wants us to believe. We tend to not want Christ alone.

Peter's defense is actually an attack on the core of what the religious leaders believed. He says what they did in healing the man was *good* — verses 8-9. And they did it in the name of Jesus, by his authority and power (verses 10-11). He says: "You rejected him by crucifying him. God approved of him by raising him from the dead."

The best apologetic for the gospel is the empty tomb. Everybody — secular or not — agrees the tomb was empty. What is the best explanation? Nobody produced the body. If the enemies of Christ had removed it, they would have said so. If the apostles had taken it, they would not have been willing to die for that lie. Christ was raised from the dead as God's approval upon his sacrifice.

Peter confirms his point in verse 11 by quoting Psalm 118:22: "The stone which the builders rejected has become the chief cornerstone." The very person the religious rulers discarded became the foundational piece on which the church is built. Remember Matthew 16: "Upon this rock" — the confession that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of the living God — "I will build my church."

## III. The All-Sufficient Savior (Acts 4:12)

But Peter doesn't stop there. Jesus is not just the stone on which things are built — he is *everything*. Verse 12: "Nor is there salvation in any other, for there is no other name under heaven given among men, by which we must be saved."

Peter declares that Jesus is the all-sufficient Savior. The name represents all that Jesus is and all that Jesus has done. But it also specifies: this name, no other name. This is the only person. This is the only work through which people can be saved.

The last recorded words of John Newton of Amazing Grace fame were written down by his young friend William Jay: "My memory is nearly gone, but I remember two things: that I am a great sinner, and that Christ is a great Savior."

The greatest decision any human ever makes concerns the nature of Christ. Jesus himself asked the Pharisees in Matthew 22:42: "What do you think about the Christ? Whose Son is he?" The same question is put before every one of us. Everything hinges on that decision — with eternity in the balance.

### Eleven Things We Add to Christ

I wanted to give you eleven things that we as average Christians add to Christ, thinking they will improve our standing with God. I had to stop at eleven. Our hearts are able to make anything something we trust more than Christ.

**First: Our own religious practices.** We trust that God will accept us because we are baptized, or because we take communion, or because we attend church regularly. Baptism is super important — but a wet head is not what you're offering to God for salvation. Church attendance is important — but if you say at the pearly gate, "I attended church regularly," those gates will be closed. Only Christ.

**Second: Obedience to man-made standards.** Checking all the boxes on a list of dos and don'ts. The only box that matters is: do you believe in Jesus Christ? The best you can bring to God — your best deed, the thing you're most proud of — is, from God's perspective, filthy rags: "All our righteousness are like filthy rags" (Isaiah 64:6). Augustus Toplady's words say it perfectly: "Nothing in my hand I bring, simply to the cross I cling."

**Third: Denominational tradition.** God is not going to accept you because you are Presbyterian or Baptist or non-denominational. Right theology is of utmost importance — but trusting in right theology is not the same as trusting in the God who gave us the right theology.

**Fourth: Family pedigree.** God does not care about your last name or who your grandparents were. Only who you are in Christ matters.

**Fifth: Our intellect.** We think God needs us because we are intelligent. But God spoke through a donkey (Numbers 22). The faith that saves is the kind of faith even a little child is capable of having.

**Sixth: Our education.** Homework for today: read 1 Corinthians 1:18 to the end of the chapter — "not many wise, not many noble are chosen."

**Seventh: Our natural abilities.** Physical abilities, athletic achievements — heaven is not gained by throwing a ball far or hard.

**Eighth: Our social media image.** Portraying perfect kids, a perfect house, perfect meals. Instagram is a lie. Social media does not save you.

**Ninth: Our possessions.** "He who dies with the most toys wins." The truth is: he who dies with the most toys still dies and immediately faces the judgment of God. The only thing that will matter at that moment is whether you are clinging to Jesus.

**Tenth: Our ethnic background.** In our culture, everything is divided by ethnicity. But there is no DEI department in heaven. There are only two races: the race of Adam, living according to the flesh, and the race of Christ, born again to believe in the Lord Jesus.

**Eleventh: Our sadness.** We think that if we live a sad, deprived life, God is more likely to accept us. But this is just penance — the arrogance of believing you can pay for your own sins in this life. Don't cling to your depression as a means of earning God's favor. Cling to Christ.

To add anything to Jesus is to reject Jesus. There is only one Savior of God's elect, and his name is Jesus Christ.

## IV. Christ Is Precious to Those Who Believe (1 Peter 2:4-7)

The same Peter who stood before the Sanhedrin later wrote in 1 Peter 2:4-7: "Coming to Him as to a living stone, rejected indeed by men, but chosen by God and precious. You also, as living stones, are being built up a spiritual house, a holy priesthood, to offer up spiritual sacrifices acceptable to God through Jesus Christ. Therefore it is also contained in the Scripture, 'Behold, I lay in Zion a chief cornerstone, elect, precious, and he who believes on Him will by no means be put to shame.' Therefore, to you who believe, He is precious."

Nothing can be added to his perfections and his completed work. In him, all our guilt is cancelled and blotted out and swallowed up, and all our sins are sunk in his precious blood.

So we keep on looking to Christ and him alone, shearing away anything we might be tempted to add to him — because after all, to live is Christ. And there is no other name under heaven by which you and I must be saved other than Jesus Christ.

Let us pray together. Lord our God, we thank you that you are a God who clearly shows us that Christ is our Savior. We pray that you would open the eyes of our hearts to believe that. And for those of us who believe, enable us to hold on to him and to see him as more beautiful and sufficient than anything we could add to him. We ask in his name, amen.
